data.title: SCHD: The AI Frenzy Is Gone
data.description:
Investment thesis

My previous bullish thesis on the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) aged decently with a 1.3% total return. However, the stock underperformed compared to the broader U.S. equity market since early October.

Despite this underperformance, I continue accumulating my position in SCHD as the latest earnings season revealed that the optimism around growth stocks is close to peak levels. President Trump's quite aggressive rhetoric on various issues is also not very good for long-term instruments like growth stocks. SCHD offers a perfect balance of dividend yield and growth, which is a good choice amid the current kind of fragile environment. The stock's dividend scorecard looks much better compared to peer large dividend ETFs. All in all, I reiterate "Strong buy" for SCHD.

Recent developments

Yesterday Nvidia (NVDA) released another impressive quarterly earnings with quite aggressive guidance. Nevertheless, there was no post-market spike as NVDA share price declined slightly. Overall, despite mostly positive earnings surprises, six out of the "Magnificent 7" stocks are currently cheaper than they were at the end of 2024.

I think that this is a clear sign for investors that the next few months might be quite bumpy for growth stocks. Such a cautious dynamic despite robust earnings suggests that optimism is at peak levels after massive rallies of 2023 and 2024. Google’s CEO stated in December 2024 that the ‘low-hanging fruit’ era for AI has passed—a view that now appears to have been accurate. This is a big reason why investors might consider rebalancing their portfolios by increasing the weight of safer assets.

It is also crucial to keep in mind that this year is also started with aggressive expansion of Chinese start-ups and large tech companies to the generative AI field. The DeepSeek R1 release shocked the American stock market, which challenged the AI leadership of the U.S. tech behemoths. There are also young and promising European players in the AI field like Nebius Group (NBIS), which also add competition to the global AI landscape.

Another big reason to be cautious on long-duration [i.e. growth stocks] stocks is the rapidly evolving geopolitical environment. Trump started his second presidential term quite aggressively and some of the initiatives are quite questionable. His trade war against China which was started during the first presidential term was more-or-less explainable by China's ambitious to become the world's economic and technological leader as well as the historical rivalry between the West and the East.

However, his second presidential term started with initiating trade war against America's historical allies and the largest trade partners like Mexico, Canada, and the European Union. I am not speaking about potential unfavorable geopolitical implications since I am not an expert in this area. What is more important in the context of the U.S. growth stocks is that increased tariffs on goods imported from the country's largest trade partners will extremely likely lead to a new spike in inflation.

Even without the effect of increased tariffs, the U.S. inflation is still higher than the Fed's target level of 2%. With inflation still running hot and vast uncertainty around Trump's next steps on tariffs, the Fed is extremely unlikely to rush with more interest rate cuts. This is another adverse short-term factor for growth stocks, meaning that valuation models will continue incorporating more aggressive discount rates.

The picture does not look beautiful with a tight monetary environment, elevated inflation expectations, and the AI frenzy gone. Thus, it is highly likely that investors will start to rebalance their portfolios by rotating from growth to value. SCHD appears to be one of the top safe haven options due to multiple reasons. The above pie chart shows us the sector-wise holdings breakdown. The top-3 sectors are all defensive, which is preferable in the current challenging environment for sectors like Technology or Communication.

In my previous SCHD thesis I explained in detail why I consider High Dividend Yield Index Fund ETF (VYM) and Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG) from Vanguard to be SCHD's main rivals. In terms of the by-sector breakdown these options look less attractive in the current unfavorable environment for growth stocks with their much less exposure to defensive sectors. The Technology sector is the number one holding for VIG and number two for VYM.

Moreover, SCHD offers a much better dividend scorecard compared to its closest rivals. The ETF is more attractive in terms of the yield and historical dividend growth. Given that frequency and consistency are almost identical for all three options, it is certain that SCHD is the highest-quality dividend machine among them.

Risks to my thesis

Trump is not only about the aggressive geopolitical rhetoric, but also about deregulation and plans to cut taxes. These are certainly positive factors for the U.S. business, and some of these steps might boost confidence in the America's stock market. Political decisions and announcements are not easily predicted, meaning that some sudden positive swings might happen as well. Moreover, decreased taxes and deregulation initiatives could mitigate the adverse effect on inflation from increased tariffs. As a result, my assertions about the more cautious Fed might not age well.

In addition to the inflation agenda, let's not forget about the importance of energy prices. The "Drill, baby, drill" agenda will boost oil and gas supply, which will weigh on global commodity prices. This is inherently disinflationary. Furthermore, we see that Trump's rhetoric on Russia is quite friendly, which might be another crucial catalyst that will weigh on energy prices. Russia is one of the largest oil and gas producers and exporters, meaning that stopping sanctions against Russian hydrocarbons will also boost global supply.

Last but not least, technological breakthroughs might also happen quite unexpectedly. The emergence of generative AI in early 2023 was sensational for the stock market, which has led to a big two-year rally for growth stocks. Tech giants invest in R&D and data centers more than ever, meaning that there is a probability of new groundbreaking technological releases.

Bottom line

To conclude, SCHD is still a "Strong buy". This looks like the best large dividend ETF option in the current environment as it offers strong exposure to defensive sectors and its dividend scorecard is by far the best compared to both VIG and VYM.

data.title: Broadcom: Margin Of Safety Still Low Despite Price Pullback
data.description:
AVGO stock: previous thesis and new developments

My last article on Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ:AVGO) stock was published back in May 2024. That article was titled "Broadcom Stock: Time To Take Some Profit". In that article, I rated the stock as a hold based on a 2-stage discounted cash flow model. Based on the results, I concluded that:

Broadcom Inc. is currently priced for perfection, leading little margin of safety. For investors truly committed to the long term, there is plenty to like, such as its focus on enterprise infrastructure, strategic M&A, and AI potential. However, in the near term, the market's expectations for its EPS growth are overly optimistic in my view.

Since then, there have been many new catalysts evolving around AVGO stock. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top two on my list: the profit drivers updated in its recent FQ4 2024 earnings report (ER) and also its ongoing external acquisition plans. My thesis for this follow-up coverage is that AVGO still leaves a thin margin of safety under current conditions despite these new growth catalysts and the recent price pull back. Thus, this article reiterates my hold rating on the stock.

AVGO stock: new catalysts and earnings outlook

Let me start with the updates provided in its recent FQ4 2024 ER. AVGO released the ER on December 12, 2024. The updates were quite up beating, delivering a strong normalized EPS of $1.42, beating estimates by $0.03. GAAP EPS came in at $0.90, surpassing expectations even more by $0.20. Revenue reached $14.05B, missing estimates by a very slight margin of $6.74M. Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter (scheduled for March 6, 2025), AVGO is projected to deliver a normalized EPS of $1.51, translating into a sequential growth of more than 6%. Also note that its EPS revisions over the last 90 days show 21 upward adjustments and only 2 downward adjustments, indicating positive analyst sentiment.

There are indeed plenty of reasons to support the positive sentiment here. Like many of other prominent semiconductor companies, AVGO is also benefiting from artificial-intelligence (AI) and the AI-related expenditure from its customers. In addition to the AI-tailwind, AVGO is simultaneously integrating its massive acquisition of VMware, too. I see good odds for hyper-scaling on this front given this unique software business and thus have a favorable view on its long-term growth perspective.

AVGO stock: growth still fully priced in

Such a favorable view is in line with consensus projections for the next few years. As you can see from the following table, consensus EPS estimates point to a promising growth trajectory for AVGO in the next 5 years. For FY 2025, the consensus EPS is projected to be $6.35, representing a 30% YOY increase. The growth is expected to fluctuate somewhat, but is expected to stay in the double digits. All told, the average growth rate for the next 5 years is projected to be about 17% in CAGR terms.

However, the stock is also expensively priced. As seen in the chart above, the forward P/E is estimated at 31.15 based on FY 2025 EPS forecast. With an average growth rate of 17% as expected, the PEG (P/E growth) ratio is more than 1.8x, far above the golden standard of 1x most GARP (growth at reasonable price) investors seek.

Admittedly, the above accounting EPS overstates its valuation multiples. As a high-growth company, AVGO spends aggressively on growth CAPEX, while its maintenance CAPEX Is relatively low. As a reflection, the next chart compares AVGO Capital Expenditures and Total Depreciation and Amortization (D&A, which I use to approximate maintenance CAPEX here). As seen, its Capital Expenditures have been ramping up quickly in recent years. Starting below $3B in 2016, it doubled to over $6B by 2020, and more than tripled to the current level of $10 B. In contrast, its D&A remained relatively low and also stable around $548M.

To get a more quantitative estimate of its growth CAPEX expenditures, I followed Bruce Greenwald's method (as detailed in his book entitled Value Investing). Growth CAPEX should be considered part of the owner's earnings (OE) following the wisdom of Warren Buffet. When the growth CAPEX is added to the OE, my analyses based on the Greenwald method showed that AVGO's OE is on average ~23% higher than its accounting EPS in the past 3 years. Thus, its P/E based on OE would be about 23% lower, resulting in a PEG ratio of about 1.5x, still far about the 1x threshold.

Other risks and final thoughts

To keep my comments more balanced, it's worthwhile pointing out the limitations of my above analysis, which translates into additional upside risks for AVGO. First, my above analysis of its OE did not factor in its share repurchase programs. The chart shows AVGO's Net Common Buyback Yield in the past. As seen, the company has been consistently buying back its own shares since 2019, and the current buyback yield is 1.32%. Such consistent buybacks add a boost to its EPS growth, especially when the shares were bought back at a discount to fair value.

Second, my above OE analysis did not factor in external acquisitions either. AVGO has a successful record of making external acquisitions. There are a few promising candidates ongoing, with the acquisition of Intel's products division as a notable one as you can see from the news below:

Seeking Alpha News: Acquiring Intel's products division would be 'extremely lucrative' for Broadcom: analyst. If Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) were to acquire Intel's (NASDAQ:INTC) products division, the potential deal would be "extremely lucrative" for the Hock Tan-led Broadcom, Piper Sandler said. "Assuming that AVGO management pays a premium to the current market cap and is able to turn this business around just as it has almost every other business that it has previously acquired, we think the numbers become extremely compelling," Piper Sandler analyst Harsh Kumar wrote in a note to clients.

All told, my overall impression is that the upside potential is in balance with the downside risks. I do see many growth catalysts from its latest ER (such as AI demand and VMware integration). But I think the elevated valuation, even after the price pull back in the past few days, already priced in these positives. As such, I don't see an obvious bias in its return/risk curve and maintain my hold rating.

data.title: Petrobras Q4 Earnings: Unpacking The Latest Concerns
data.description:
On February 20, I posted a Petrobras (PBR) earnings preview piece where I made it clear that the main topic of conversation on earnings day would be around the Brazil-based oil giant's ability to distribute robust dividends and especially extraordinary ones (i.e. beyond the regular ordinary dividends that Petrobras historically distributes).

As many investors seek to invest in Petrobras mostly because of its dividend appeal, I believed that much of the bullish or bearish outlook would be around this agenda, and so, with uncertain talk of extraordinary dividends, I decided at the time to adopt a position of staying on the sidelines, which turned out to be the right decision.

Here's exactly what I wrote:

“The main topic of conversation on earnings day, however, is likely to be around the distribution of dividends and the possibility of extraordinary dividends. The consensus for the quarter is for a dividend per share ranging from R$0.89 to R$1.48, or $0.15 to $0.25, which would yield a quarterly yield of between 2.3% and 3.9%.”

Assuming that many investors are drawn to the company primarily for its dividends, even if in the short term the distribution remains robust, in the long term, I see a deterioration in this pattern in the long term, which is bearish. This is especially concerning given that Brazil's equity risk premium stands at 7.6%. That is, with a dividend yield closer to this level, the risk return becomes less and less appealing.

If, on the one hand, Q4 earnings get a boost from extraordinary dividends, serving as a significant short-term catalyst for the company, on the other hand, I expect a proportionally bearish reaction if that doesn’t happen."

It turns out that I was wrongly optimistic in the short term awaiting an extraordinary dividend announcement, mainly because Brazil's federal government is facing a fiscal crisis and, as the company's main shareholder, would benefit from distributing dividends to strengthen its cash flow.

But it turns out that things ended up going in the opposite direction to my assumption, with Petrobras opting to step on the CapEx accelerator and push the focus on dividends even further to the sidelines.

Although the 4Q24 results are not necessarily a signal for panic (for current ADR holders that carry long-term profitable positions), the points of attention raised certainly cannot be ignored or softened with arguments that dividends and valuations remain attractive, in my opinion. It's true that in 4Q24 Petrobras' results were negatively impacted by exchange rate fluctuations, but there was an increase in leverage, weakening cash generation, uncertainties over CapEx anticipation, and lately, lower and shrinking dividends, the core of the company's investment thesis.

On the other hand, shares now trade at 19% down over the last twelve months, and 10% down over the last five trading days.

But also, even if earnings are declining (whether recurring or not), Petrobras today trades at an earnings yield of 19% versus 21% prior 4Q24 in January and much less attractive than the nearly 30% in April 2024. In other words, despite the falling share price, Petrobras isn't necessarily any cheaper now than it was in 3Q24.

For this reason, even if there are some possible catalysts for 1Q25 and full-year 2025, I am adopting an underperform stance for Petrobras shares post-4Q24, where the thesis seems to be stuck in terms of dividends growth and valuations that are not necessarily more attractive, which is ultimately what has mattered most to the majority of Petrobras shareholders.

Key Points of Concern Raised After 4Q Earnings

The headline on Petrobras' 4Q24 earnings came out like this on Bloomberg:

"Brazil's state-controlled oil company Petrobras plunged after it reported a surprise fourth-quarter loss and announced dividends that fell significantly short of expectations."

The bearish reaction in the share price immediately after the release of the results highlights the key issues investors were focused on. The market is digesting points of caution, even though that doesn't represent a drastic break from the excellent results that Petrobras has been delivering since 2017/2018—but it does mark a shift.

Starting with the fact that Petrobras reported a net loss to shareholders of—R$17 billion ($2.76 billion) and a net income of $7.5 billion in 2024 versus $24.9 billion in 2023. However, it is worth noting that the impact of the currency devaluation, which entered the financial result line, is only of an accounting nature related to the debts of Petrobras and its subsidiaries abroad. Excluding these effects, net income would result in R$17.8 billion ($3.09 billion) in 4Q24 and $19.4 billion in 2024.

The impact of the devaluation of the real against the dollar generated an accounting loss, since part of the company's debt is in dollars. Nevertheless, this impact is not necessarily bad for companies that trade commodities in dollars.

The weaker numbers than the market anticipated in terms of the bottom line came from EBITDA. This worsening is related to higher-than-expected costs, with EBITDA in the E&P line, Petrobras' main business focus, falling by 44.7% YoY.

Therefore, 4Q24 was a quarter marked by (1) tighter costs and expenses, (2) a more compressed Brent price, (3) lower E&P production, (4) refining margins still operating at levels of limited efficiency (8% in 4Q24 vs. historical 10%) and (5) structurally higher CAPEX, given the current mandate and more aggressive strategic plan with investments.

The key points to watch, however, are:

(1) Petrobras' indebtedness has increased, reaching the highest level of the last two years with a net debt/EBITDA LTM adjusted of 1.29x in 2024 vs. 0.85x in 2023 (see chart below);

(2) free cash flow of $23.3 billion in 2024 vs. $31 billion in 2023, an annual drop of 25% where part of this reduction is due to the increase in investments (CapEx) made by Petrobras and; (3) the same CapEx that Petrobras anticipated investments, especially in the Búzios basin. Although this is a very profitable reserve, the anticipation of these investments put pressure on cash flow in 4Q24 (down by 45.1% versus 4Q23) and may raise doubts about the profitability of these projects.

The last, but much less important, point that deserves special mention is the lower dividend—which is also a reflection of the higher indebtedness and the reduction in cash generation. It's worth noting that Petrobras' dividend policy is to distribute minimum mandatory dividends corresponding to 25% of net profit, and if gross debt is less than US$ 60 billion, the company can distribute 45% of its free cash flow.

Therefore, gross debt will end up at the limit in 2024 at $60.31 billion. In 2024 Petrobras distributed less dividends compared to last year, being $18.32 billion (versus $19.67 billion in 2023), approximately 78% of FCF being in 4Q24, an amount of $5.45 billion, without having had the distribution of extraordinary dividends in the quarter.

Why Things Are Looking Bad for Petrobras

Let's start with dividends.

In the last twelve months, Petrobras has had an incredible dividend yield of ~20%. For 2025, 2026 and 2027, the consensus implies a yield of 14%, 12.3% and 12.8%, respectively. Petrobras' CEO Magda Chambriard declared that it is "stepping on the accelerator" with the company investing in oil equipment and services, such as expanding a fleet of support vessels and expanding refining capacity.

Considering that investing in Brazil implies an equity risk premium of 7.6%, it's undeniable that the yield levels for PBR should remain at least robust—even with free cash flow being allocated to accelerating CapEx (investments) to increase production. But on the other hand, a decrease in dividend growth rates certainly doesn't sound bullish.

Additionally, regarding the investments, there are many inherent risks: (1) execution; (2) increased refining capacity, in a company that openly does not follow international parity; (3) a history of state corruption along these lines.

But going deeper into these investments, I want to take a closer look at the Equatorial Margin (which could even be a major risk to the bearish thesis).

This is Brazil's new oil frontier, stretching from the states of Rio Grande do Norte to Amapá, in the northeast and north of the country. Petrobras will invest $3 billion by 2029 to drill 15 wells, with a potential of 5-7.5 billion recoverable barrels, more than the pre-salt. At first, the estimated production in 2030 and beyond could sustain Petrobras for decades, at low cost ($20-25/barrel).

Petrobras is currently awaiting the environmental license from "Ibama" exploring the Foz do Amazonas in Q1 2025, and the Brazilian President Lula da Silva is pushing the environmental agency for supporting the exploration. It's worth noting that Ibama denied the license in 2023, facing resistance from indigenous communities and federal prosecutors.

In other words, Ibama's license in 1Q25 or 2Q25 could be a major catalyst for Petrobras shares, but this is highly speculative.

Another speculative point is that the U.S. dollar versus the Brazilian real dropped in 1Q25 and possibly, there will be no non-recurring events, so earnings should take a hit in 1Q (even without the cash effect). This could be a premise for an extraordinary dividend for 1Q25 or sometime in 2025. However, FX is another point that gets out of hand, and adopting a stance that the BRL will appreciate versus the USD is even more speculative, given all the fiscal crisis that Brazil has been facing throughout 2024 and is likely to face in 2025.

Finally, Petrobras has managed to readjust prices in line with the international price parity (IPP) of fuels.

In February, the average price charged by Petrobras refineries was R$3.06/liter for gasoline and R$3.84/liter for diesel. Thus, the prices charged by Petrobras refineries are practically in line with international parity, with a premium of 0.3% on gasoline (+R$0.01/liter) and in line with the price of diesel on the same basis.

This was great news for PBR investors since the discontinuation of this parity policy by Petrobras since 2023 requires a readjustment in fuel prices so that the company does not lose profitability. But it remains to be seen how consistent Petrobras will be in 2025 in keeping prices in line with international parity, without the Brazilian government interference, amid a scenario of high economic instability.

Final Remarks

I've been a harsh critic of Petrobras throughout my coverage of the company's ADR.

In large part, my skepticism rests on the premise that in order to continue generating alpha, Petrobras will need to maintain its appealing dividend thesis. In 2024, Petrobras delivered a total return to shareholders of ~20% considering the share price depreciation (-21%) and the payment of dividends (yielding ~20.8%).

The bar for shareholder returns has been set very high in the past couple of years, and it has left many investors overly demanding. This has also been enough to overshadow any concerns about the political risks tied to the company’s investment thesis.

Although the current yield and projections for the next two years are still arguably robust, the dividend distribution is a devolution versus previous years' levels, including 2024.

Additionally, as the company is in a stage of significant investments to expand production, free cash flow is likely to remain under pressure and persistent FX headwinds may put extra pressure on debt levels, and I fear that this may be enough to leave investors uneasy.

That said, I don't see the bearish reaction of the markets around PBR after 4Q24 as an exaggeration, but rather as the consolidation of a bearish pattern—something I wasn’t so sure about before 4Q24. For that reason, I'm updating my stance on PBR to underperform (which may sound contrarian for some). Even though Petrobras has one of the largest and highest-quality reserves in the world, at the end of the day, the company's strategy is too focused on shareholder returns—and the trend is leaning bearish.

data.title: Worldline 2024 Earnings Review: Value Or Trap?
data.description:
Worldline (OTCPK:WWLNF) has recently reported its 2024 earnings, showing a weak operating performance and its outlook for 2025 was also not much strong. Despite that, a new CEO was appointed, and a strategy update is expected during the second half of the year, which may be important catalysts for a higher valuation in the future.

As I’ve covered in previous articles, Worldline’s financial performance has been quite weak over the past couple of years, something that is justified both by external factors and some fundamental issues. This has resulted in a very weak share price performance over the past three years, having the worst performance compared to its closest peers Nexi (OTCPK:NEXXY) and Adyen(OTCPK:ADYEY), as shown in the next graph. Its shares have lost about 86% of their value since the beginning of 2022 (white line in the graph), compared to -59% for Nexi and a smaller decline (-7%) for Adyen during this period.

Due to this weak performance, Worldline’s current market value is only about $1.8 billion, being much lower than Nexi ($6 billion market value) or Adyen ($57 billion). While some part of this negative performance can be justified by a de-rating of payments stocks following the pandemic boost, when Worldline and other payment companies performed quite well, there are also some fundamental reasons for this negative performance in recent years.

Indeed, Worldline has struggled to adapt its business to technological developments and integration of payments in more flexible solutions, such as cloud-based solutions for merchants from other fintech startups or Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools implemented by competitors like Adyen, being a key reason why Worldline’s revenues and earnings have been under pressure over the past couple of years.

While the company acknowledges that it needs to invest in technology and offer better solutions for its customers, it takes some time to turnaround the company and recent operational performance has remained somewhat weak. Competition remains intense in the digital payments industry and companies that do not adapt in the merchant’s market may be at risk of becoming obsolete. Worldline is clearly among the companies that are struggling in the industry, given that its product offering is a collection of legacy systems that only cover specific needs of merchants instead of providing a full-service product among the payments cycle.

This puts the company at a competitive disadvantage that is not easy to fix in the short term, explaining for instance why Worldline has recently entered a partnership with OPP to provide an embedded payments solution in Europe. Worldline was not competitive in this payment segment, thus a partnership with OPP allowed it to offer a more competitive solution for customers, with around 28 million of sellers already being onboard.

On top of providing new products or services to customers and investing in technological developments, the company also started a cost cutting program during 2024, aiming to make its operations more efficient and protect business margins.

Despite these efforts, Worldline’s operating performance has not been great in recent quarters and the company has made three profit warnings in just one year, leading to the exit of its CEO last September. Investors should be aware that Worldline’s CEO hold that role for 11 years, thus this was to some extent an unexpected move. It also shows that Worldline clearly needs to change its business for a turnaround to be possible in the near future, as management was not showing a great capacity to improve the company’s competitive position rapidly.

As the CEO was not replaced soon, its Investor Day originally scheduled for last November was postponed, and Worldline only announced a new CEO in recent days, together with its 2024 earnings release. The new CEO comes from the French bank Group BPCE, having a long experience in the payments industry. Worldline’s board of directors acknowledged the company needed a new external perspective and that was a key reason to select a CEO coming from another company. The new CEO was head of payments in the digital and payments division of Group BPCE since 2018, and previously worked at Ingenico from 2009 to 2017, a company that was coincidentally bought by Worldline back in 2020.

The new CEO is expected to be an important driver of transformation for Worldline in the near future and the company is expected to review its business strategy in the coming months. An investor day is expected to be performed during the second half of 2025, when management is expected to present a new business strategy and explain to investors and the financial community how it intends to turnaround the business.

While nothing seems to be closed at the moment, Worldline is expected to sell non-core units, namely its Mobility and e-Transational Services (MeTS) segment, and focus its business on the merchants and financial services segments. Worldline has received some expressions of interest for its MeTS segment and if pricing for this deal is good for the company, a sale may be closed in the coming months.

For the remaining business, operating momentum has not been great in recent quarters and the company’s outlook is also somewhat weak in the short term.

Indeed, Worldline’s revenues amounted to slightly above €4.6 billion in 2024, representing an organic increase of only 0.5% YoY. In the second half of 2024, organic revenues declined by 1%, being below estimates, showing that despite the company’s efforts to improve its business by terminating some merchant accounts in Germany or taking pricing actions in Australia, this was not enough to offset overall weakness across the business. Investors should note that Worldline’s initial guidance was to report annual growth of about 2-3%, which was revised downward by the middle of the year to 1% growth. Worldline was expecting Q3 2024 to be a turning point for revenue growth, but this has not happened, and its guidance is also soft for the first semester of 2025, expecting now a turnaround on revenue growth only in the second half of 2025.

It expects a return to stronger revenue growth by investing in its product offering, new client wins, the start of some joint ventures in France and Italy, and possibly by gaining some market share. However, execution will be key for a recovery to revenue growth in the coming quarters, something that is not progressing well as the company had some hardware delays recently, which puts further pressure on revenue growth.

Regarding costs, the company is doing a better job as this is a factor where it has more control. In 2024, it was able to reduce costs by €117 million and is progressing well to achieve annual cost savings of €220 million by the end of 2025. On capital expenditures and integration costs, the company was also able to achieve some savings during 2024, with capex lower 15% YoY and integration costs declined by €50 million in the year. These actions were quite important for the company to protect its free cash flow generation, which amounted to €201 million in the year and was in-line with its guidance.

For 2025, its guidance appears to be quite underwhelming given that Worldline is expecting similar revenue growth rate compared to 2024 and some growth in free cash flow before financial costs.

Regarding its balance sheet, net debt amounted to €2 billion at the end of 2024, representing a leverage ratio of 1.9x (net debt-to-EBITDA ratio). This is an acceptable leverage level, but for a technology company with good business margins and free cash flow generation its leverage could be lower, and this is another area where the company needs to improve in the near term, even though it’s not a concern for the time being.

Given this challenging backdrop and quite muted growth expectations for 2025, it’s not surprising to see that Worldline continues to trade at depressed valuation multiples. Indeed, its shares are currently trading at only 3.6x forward earnings, showing that a lot of issues are already reflected in its share price. However, a turnaround is not certain in the short term and uncertainty about the company’s strategy should be a key overhand in the coming months. New management has a very difficult job to regain investor confidence ahead, even though upside could be considerable if its operating momentum improves or if, eventually, someone tries to buy the company.

There has been speculation in the recent past that Bain Capital, a private equity company, could be interested in buying Worldline, something that could make sense as the valuation is certainly depressed right now. Indeed, other companies in the payments industry trade at much higher valuation multiples, with the only exception being Nexi which is also trading at a depressed valuation. However, Nexi is trading closer to 7x earnings, which is almost double compared to Worldline, showing that investor sentiment toward Worldline is quite negative right now.

Conclusion

While Worldline has struggled to grow its business in recent quarters and a rebound is not certain in the short term, the company is still profitable, generates cash flow, and has an acceptable financial position. However, looking at its valuation investors could consider that the company was in financial distress, which is certainly not the case. Its new CEO has a difficult job ahead to turnaround the business, but market expectations are so low that it seems easy for its share price to rebound if operating momentum improves in the coming quarters. On top of that, its depressed valuation could also lead Worldline to be a takeover target in the future, which would certainly be done at a premium to its current value. Given this background, I think Worldline is an interesting speculative value play in the European payments industry, even though investors need to have patience as a turnaround will certainly take some time to improve the company’s fundamentals.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Jane's January 2025 Retirement Income Update: A Conservative Forecast Of Income Growth
data.description:
I'd be lying if I didn't say that I hate doing forecasts. Forecasting is one of those things where you might dislike it, but that doesn't mean that it's not necessary.

On a similar note, I have been installing new doors, door frames, and stained wood trim in my house, which requires a considerable amount of sanding. Long hours of sanding are about the closest thing I can think of to being universally despised, but it is one of those activities that is clearly revealed in the final product, as there is no substitute for the level of effort and attention to detail needed to make the final product beautiful.

The challenge with forecasting is that the line between being too simplistic in your analysis and exploring every possible rabbit hole isn't as far off as one might think. The problem with an analysis being too simplistic isn't that it's wrong, it's that it's just plain boring and frankly, explaining something that is just apparent to the average person really doesn't make for a compelling write-up. On the other hand, getting overly complicated is an error I tend to gravitate towards can easily drown out what may be the most important points of conversation by including unlikely scenarios or overly complicated topics.

In the analysis last year, I decided to create tables that help explain the most important points about the growth of John and Jane's income while isolating the variables to provide clarity on where some of the shifts are coming from. For example, CD's and Charles Schwab Money Market (SWVXX) generated a whopping $7,131.28 of the income generated in Jane's Traditional and Roth IRAs in 2024 (but looking back to 2023 tables I wouldn't have been able to easily see how much this had grown because I only tracked it on a monthly basis and not annually). Total income from this category in 2023 was around $1500 in the same two accounts, which means it increased five-fold year-over-year.

Trying to predict when variables like this are going to occur is by far the most difficult part of the forecasting discussion since this involves pulling out a crystal ball and knowing what interest rates were going to do and how that would have an impact on John and Jane's investments. Thankfully, interest rate increases by the Federal Reserve were about as clear of a broadcast as we could get that the low interest rate environment was rapidly changing.

I expect that we will see income from CDs and SWVXX reduced considerably in FY-2025. Looking at the year to come, I expect interest rates to remain flat. As a result of this, CD's and other fixed-income instruments will be less attractive, especially because financial institutions don't seem panicked about needing to meet growing loan demand that was inflated by artificially low interest rates from the Federal Reserve.

So what does this mean for Jane's income in 2025?

The Traditional IRA was (and is still currently) highly dependent on income generated by CD's and SWVXX. Jane the following certificates of deposit that are set to expire in 2025:

  • $25,000 - 5.45% - expires on March 26, 2025.
  • $30,000 - 4.45% - expires on May 30, 2025.

These two sources currently generate about $230/month for the portfolio while SWVXX generates around $184/month.

On the table for the Traditional IRA it currently reflects a -14% impact to Jane's income compared to FY-2024 while the Roth IRA reflects -.9%. The Roth IRA does not currently have any CDs, and it's only fixed-income source is SWVXX.

There are a couple of options we have to consider as the expiration dates get closer (there are additional options, but I am leaving those out to make sure we don't muddy the waters of this conversation).

  1. We can renew at lower CD rates. This will tie up the money and guarantee the yield while potentially resulting in reduced income in the event that rates were to go up.
  2. Shift the funds from CDs to SWVXX. This keeps the funds very liquid but subjects the funds to a variable rate yield (currently 4.19%) which may result in underperformance if rates keep falling from where they currently are.
  3. We can invest in stocks and other tradeable securities but with the market at all times highs and the potential of tariffs we could see an impact to earnings and potential dividend increases.

The likelihood is that Jane will likely utilize elements of all three options in an effort to take a balanced approach to the challenge at hand. Ultimately, the goal is to protect current income levels and find investments that allow growth in future income levels. Readers can expect this discussion to be a common theme in the John and Jane articles for the 2025 season.

Lastly, it's important to remember that these forecasts are intended to be overly conservative in nature because I do not like having to backtrack reckless estimates. As we obtain the income from each passing month, I expect to see the expected income grow, thus reducing the shortfall and will likely turn into income growth.

Dividend Increases

December had a total of seven holdings that increased their dividend payout or provided a special dividend.

  • Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) - Increased dividend by 1.5%.
  • Avient (AVNT) - Increased dividend by 4.9%.
  • CME Group (CME) - Variable annual dividend increased by 10.5%.
  • EOG Resources (EOG) - Increased dividend by 7.1%.
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN) - Increased dividend by 2.0%.
  • Realty Income (O) - Increased dividend by .2%.
  • WP Carey (WPC) - Increased dividend by .6%.

January Trades - Traditional IRA

There were fewer trades made in the Traditional IRA Account during the month of January than in December. I won't cover all the trades, but will focus on some of the more compelling trades that have taken place.

British American Tobacco (BTI) - it's not very common that you see a purchase made because the stock price is attractive and then two weeks later a sale made because the stock price hit a limit threshold that was set. That's exactly what happened with trades of BTI. We now have more limit trades in place to purchase shares if it drops below $36/share.

Unilever (UL) - UL recently saw its stock price push to a 52-week-high at the end of Q3 2024 and has since relapsed back to the mid $50/share range. The main reason for closing out this position favored the opportunity to invest in Kimberly-Clark De Mexico (OTCPK:KCDMY) for stronger return potential and a more compelling dividend yield.

Kimberly-Clark De Mexico - Even with the potential complications of tariffs, there is a compelling dividend yield and growth potential for shares of KCDMY moving forward. Historically, the stock has traded between a range of $6-$12/share over the last decade. We expect we will be adding more shares in the future, especially with the recent pullback in price.

January Trades - Roth IRA

There was only one trade made in Jane's Roth IRA during the month of January.

Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) - Despite the potential threat of tariffs, CNQ presents a compelling valuation at current prices (which are now $3/share less than they were at the time of purchase). CNQ has been extremely shareholder-friendly and prudent with its capital allocation. I believe there are good reasons to continue adding more shares at these levels.

Portfolio Composition

The images below are focused on what is happening now and moving forward.

The first image shows what has happened year-over-year with the portfolio in terms of what holdings are generating income. I made the change part way through 2024 to highlight the companies where the payout increased as a result of a special dividend or dividend increase. This is designated by the color orange.

Below is an updated table of the extremely conservative forecast that suggests income will be down -13.2% in the Traditional IRA and down -.2% in the Roth IRA for FY-2024.

I have added the third table to show the combined numbers and created a new column to give a better perspective on the monthly change when it comes to year-over-year income, which is expected to be down -9.0% overall.

The next two images have been updated from my forecasting articles to look at how much the income has grown on a monthly basis and separate out what income is from dividends and what comes from CDs/Money Markets, etc.

This offers more insight/context as to where Jane's income is being derived (equities, fixed income, etc.). These are also more realistic numbers of what we expect to see for income growth, and also give us the ability to better track which months have the largest impact.

Here is a basic understanding of what each of the columns/fields means:

  • 2023 - Income results for 2023
  • 2024 (Ex CDs) - Estimated income 2024 (yellow) and actual income 2024 (green) but excludes all income earned from money markets, CDs, etc.
  • 2024 (W CDs) - Estimated CD & money market income 2024 (yellow) and actual income 2024 (green). Separated because we want to differentiate between dividend income and growth compared to CDs/money markets that are not subject to increases.
  • Total Income - Combined total income from dividends, CDs, Money market, etc.
  • 3%/5%/7% Increase - These columns serve as a marker so you can see how much income would need to be hit that month to achieve a specific increase. I will likely change these numbers to be more account-specific in the future (some accounts are growing more rapidly than others).

It is worth nothing that the original forecast tables reflected an expected reduction of income at -14.0% and -.9% (these are the tables back in the introduction). With the update of January's income, these numbers have improved to -13.2% and -.2%, respectively. Even though January's income was not an "increase" year-over-year, it does mean that the amount of income collected was higher than originally anticipated. We expect to see these numbers steadily move in a positive direction over the next 12 months.

Conclusion

This year, I have decided to use the conclusion section to reflect on the change in account balances in these accounts. It is important to remember that Jane does not contribute any funds to her retirement accounts, nor does she take anything out. I decided to also keep 2023 balances to give a better perspective than just a year-over-year comparison.

Although this isn't necessarily considered a dazzling performance to a growth investor, it has come with a substantial amount of income growth. Thankfully, I also have the receipts on that to show that the income growth generated has exploded over the previous seven years.

With the state of the economy and the fragile standing of the average person's finances, I believe that this year will be a weaker year for growth. In the Traditional IRA, we will also need to address the issue of reallocating the large certificates of deposit that are coming due, which may also have short-term ramifications.

Our forecast is intended to be conservative (as has every prior forecast I have completed on SA) but so far, Jane has never logged a negative year of dividend growth in her portfolios and the majority of years reflecting double-digit growth when it comes to income.

What stocks are you seeing that aren't in Jane's Retirement portfolio? Are there any compelling investments I should be looking at? I would love to hear any ideas/suggestions in the comment section.

John and Jane are long all holdings mentioned in this article.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: A Volatile Share Price Masks Some Of The Progress At Inogen
data.description:
A new management team and a new business strategy are producing results at Inogen (NASDAQ:INGN) as this small-cap manufacturer of portable oxygen concentrators looks to forge a more stable business model by prioritizing sales to durable/home medical equipment providers and a growing rental business. While there is still a long way to go before the turnaround is truly complete, 2024 results were better than expected, and it doesn’t take heroic assumptions to drive a higher fair value.

Inogen’s share price performance since my last update has been volatile to say the least. The shares traded to (and slightly above) my $12 fair value, but each time they ran up, they came plunging back down into the $8’s. I still think $12 is a realistic fair value for the next 12 months or so, but there is certainly the potential to exceed that level if and when management can stabilize the direct-to-consumer (or DTC) business, continue growing the B2B business, and leverage the market potential of the recently approved Simeox system.

A Good End To A Better-Than-Expected Year

Inogen closed the 2024-year on a good note, despite ongoing challenges in stabilizing the DTC business at a sustainable profitable level. B2B sales remain healthy and ASPs seem to have stabilized, while there has been meaningful progress on margins.

Revenue rose about 5% in constant currency terms, beating by 8% (the company previewed their revenue results, so I’m using the sell-side estimates before that release), and while revenue declined sequentially, that’s normal (and the 10% decline was actually a fair bit better than it’s been in recent years).

Total sales revenue rose 12% yoy, with units up 13% and ASPs stabilizing after three consecutive quarters of double-digit declines. Domestic B2B sales rose 24% yoy, while OUS sales rose 28%. DTC sales declined 21% as the company continues to reduce rep headcount in pursuit of a sustainable headcount. Rental revenue declined 16% yoy.

Gross margin eased slightly on a sequential basis (down 120bp) but improved 820bp to 45.3% and beat expectations by more than a point. The company is still loss-making, but adjusted EBITDA loss narrowed to less than $4M (on $80M in revenue), beating expectations, and the company did manage a small positive adjusted EBITDA in the two prior quarters.

All told, 2024 was a positive year for Inogen and one that supported management’s new strategic direction for the business. While DTC sales were consistently weak, and weaker than expected, throughout the year, the B2B sales effort saw better than expected growth, leading to a full-year tally that was about 5% better than I’d expected, as well as a smaller EBITDA loss ($9M better than I’d expected) and a smaller cash burn ($11M better).

Guidance Disappoints, But The Reaction Seems Overdone

Inogen’s fourth quarter results were better than expected, but guidance for 2025 didn’t go over particularly well, driving a 15% drop in the share price.

Management guided to FY’25 revenue growth of 5% to 6%, with the midpoint of the guidance range ($353.5M) about 2% above where the Street was at prior to earnings. Management acknowledged that B2B sales momentum is going to slow as it annualizes against tougher comps, and the DTC is still looking for its water line as the company continues to adjust headcount with an eye toward sustainable profitability in that part of the business.

I believe it was the P&L guidance that drove the disappointment. Gross margin guidance of 43% to 45% is a noticeable step-down from the 46.1% achieved for all of 2024, and I think the Street/investors were surprised by the 100bp impact of the Simeox launch spending and the initiation of the strategic agreement with Yuwell, the impact of higher-cost inventory running through the books in 2025, and the lack of more margin leverage in the B2B business. Given that I expected better than 46% gross margin in FY’25, I’m a little disappointed as well, but the explanations do at least make sense.

Recent Strategic Developments Have Some Potential

Inogen has seen a couple of significant business developments in the past couple of months that have meaningful implications for 2025 and beyond.

First is the December FDA approval of the company’s Simeox airway clearance device for patients' bronchial clearance issues. I’ve talked about Simeox before, but basically, it’s a device that automates the process of clearing mucus from the lungs of people with cystic fibrosis, COPD, bronchiectasis, or other conditions where excess mucus collects in the lungs and impairs breathing.

Management will start a limited launch in 2025, but the real gating issue now is getting adequate reimbursement. I don’t believe management has publicly disclosed their pricing intentions for Simeox, but similar devices retail for thousands of dollars, so private pay out of pocket really isn’t a valid option here. While there are compelling arguments for Simeox and enough patients out there to support meaningful revenue, it takes time to secure reimbursement, and particularly for smaller companies with more limited resources.

The second major development was the January announcement of a strategic partnership with China’s Jiangsu Yuyue Medical Equipment (002223.SZ) (“Yuwell”). Under this agreement Yuwell will distribute Inogen’s POCs in China (under Inogen’s brand), while Inogen will distribute Yuwell’s stationary concentrators in the U.S. (also under Inogen’s brand). The two companies will also collaborate on future R&D and Yuwell agreed to purchase 2.6M shares for $27.2M in a PIPE transaction that will give Yuwell a 9.9% stake.

Yuwell is a legitimate partner for Inogen, one of the largest Chinese domestic medical device companies, and one with meaningful exposure to respiratory care in China. While affordability will definitely be a limiting factor for Inogen POCs in the PRC, it’s still a large enough market to be worthwhile. Moreover, I like the diversification into stationary concentrators for Inogen. The deal probably won’t make a major positive impact in 2025, but I think this is a good low-risk transaction for Inogen and the buy-in from Yuwell should help incentivize their sales efforts.

The Outlook

With a better-than-expected 2024 and decent momentum from the fourth quarter, my numbers for 2025 and beyond do go up some, but the margin headwinds in 2025 mitigate some of those benefits. I’m looking for around 5% to 6% revenue growth over the next three to five years and long-term revenue growth around 5%.

There’s certainly an upside to those figures. I believe POCs can (and should) replace oxygen tanks over time, but DMEs have invested considerable resources into their oxygen tank businesses, so it will take time to convince them to switch over. Likewise, I think there’s attractive potential for Simeox, but it’s a different sales channel for Inogen and a market with different dynamics that will take time to develop. Last but not least, I’m not ignoring the potential of the Yuwell partnership, but here too Rome will not be built in a day.

On the margin side, I believe positive EBITDA is possible in 2026, and I think the company could get back to double-digit EBITDA in four or five years. The B2B business will have lower intrinsic margins than the prior DTC-based model, but there should still be attractive margin leverage as the business scales up. I’m still looking for high single-digit long-term FCF margins and there could be upside there, but I want to see how the B2B scales up before getting more aggressive. Liquidity really isn't an issue here. The company ended the year with $117M in net cash and a modest $11M negative free cash flow for the year (the second year in a row of smaller cash burn), and the Yuwell deal will bring in about $26M. While investments to support these new ventures could drive negative free cash flow in FY'25, that's not my expectation today and I don't think Inogen will need further funding unless and until the business recovery plan fails.

I’m sticking with my prior fair value range of around $12, supported by both discounted cash flow and a 0.5x multiple on forward revenue. There is definitely room for that multiple to expand, but it’s important to remember that low-growth (revenue growth below 10%) small-cap medical device stocks almost always trade at a discount to the wider sector, and particularly when profitability is low.

The Bottom Line

I think 2024 was an important step forward for Inogen in convincing investors that the new business plan is viable. That said, margin leverage is still not where it needs to be, and I expect the Street to remain concerned about whether revenue can accelerate beyond the mid-single-digits on any sustained basis and/or when double-digit EBITDA margins become viable again.

I do still see a lot of operating risk in the model, and execution on the B2B opportunity (as well as the Simeox launch and Yuwell partnership) is far from assured. With the shares back around $8, I like this as an interesting speculation, but I want to underline that this is a stock with well above-average risks, including the risk that revenue never accelerates to a point where the market would really embrace the name and award a better multiple.

data.title: Smallcap Eyecare Pharma Company Harrow Health Has Two Major Catalysts For 2025
data.description:
Harrow Health (NASDAQ:HROW) is a founder-led eye health company with a roughly $1B market capitalization that has seen strong growth in recent years due to savvy acquisitions. The stock got a little ahead of itself in 2024 and operational issues for Q3 caused the shares to fall back to what is likely now an attractive entry point.

The company has a robust distribution platform for eye care products and a range of products under patent that create a growing moat. There are multiple catalysts in 2025 chiefly from the re-launch of Triesence at a more realistic price point and the potential IPO for pharma company Melt, which HROW owns 46% of and could perhaps be a billion dollar company assuming NDA approval.

The Moat

HROW's moat is two-fold. First, many of their products are under patent or branded. Second, they have a distribution platform and relationships with 15,000 eye care specialists. These strengths are reinforcing, as HROW expands its portfolio of brands, so it can distribute them to its existing clients. These clients also benefit from a relationship with HROW for their product needs and where HROW offers a product in a category, they are more likely to purchase it.

Melt Pharma Is A Hidden Asset

HROW created Melt Pharmaceuticals and owns a 46% equity stake, a right of first refusal on commercialization and a 5% royalty interest. Melt reported a positive readout for its phase 3 trial for MELT-300 in November.

The product is an oral pill that provides sedation for cataract surgery. The novel aspects are the pill form, avoiding the need for any needles and that the product is non-opioid, reducing addiction risks. The pill delivery format can also be faster than setting up an IV, helping throughput efficiency for eye surgery. The primary use case is for cataract surgery today, but over time MELT-300 could meet other sedation needs across a range of surgeries if successful.

Melt still has some studies to complete before an NDA filling (hepatic and renal impairment studies, thorough QTc, and pivotal PK study) but those could occur this year, making NDA approval in early 2026 and perhaps launch in mid/late 2026 a realistic possibility.

Market sizing is of course probabilistic, but with a 75% chance of an approval given strong phase 3 data, 5 million cataract surgeries in the U.S. and a $150 achieved price point then with 10% market share this is a $75M revenue product. There's a pipeline and revenue growth opportunities too, so if successful, Melt could be a $1B company on a rough basis (with a range of plausible outcomes of perhaps $0-$3B). The important thing is that an IPO could happen on a 12-month view, and that could crystalize roughly $500M of value for HROW or about half of HROW's current valuation.

Clearly, there are many risks here, chiefly lack of approval and problems with commercialization. Nonetheless, Melt is no longer an early-stage pharma company and tangible evidence of success could come in late 2025 with NDA approval.

Risks From The Bear Case

The bear case on HROW often has the following points. First off, the company has grown relatively fast fueled, in part, by acquisition and net income has been broadly negative to Q3 2024 as growing operating costs have cancelled out growth in gross margin. That presents a number of common risks such as overpaying for future deals and accounting conventions masking a structurally unprofitable business. Some also view the CEO as overly promotional, for example he has a $1B revenue target for 2027. In addition, maybe the product portfolio is not sufficiently differentiated and close to end of life with patent expiry issues.

Rebutting The Bear Case

To counter these points (though some have merit in my view), I believe HROW's growth is real, the company has demonstrated both organic and inorganic growth (see ImprismisRX for something the company has created organically and is achieving tens of millions of revenue) and appears more opportunistic than reliant on acquisitions. I'll address accounting in more detail with "Assessing Factor Grades" below as it is a complex topic.

The CEO is basically the founder of the business, running it for 14 years. He owns roughly 10% of the stock as of April 2024, so that would appear to create an alignment of incentives. I don't love his $1B 2027 revenue target. In a sense, it's good to have aspirational goals, but I think there's plenty of HROW upside without meeting that target, which feels like a little too aggressive to communicate to markets without a firm plan.

Then I do think it's fair that certain of HROW's products are not well differentiated, but that's the strength of the platform-based strategy, because HROW has strong customer relationships, it's possible for HROW to sell certain products that are less differentiated and achieve a reasonable margin in the context of a broader client relationship and platform structure.

Assessing Factor Grades

Looking at HROW's quantitative factor grades. Clearly, valuation is a concern as are negative revisions after a soft Q3. Nonetheless, the strong margin and growth profile of the business is recognized.

The poor valuation grade stems from weak EBITDA, EBIT and cashflow compared to the healthcare sector. That's because even with revenue growth, SG&A expense has scaled too, meaning that materially higher gross profit from new products has not resulted in profitability. For example, for the 9 months to September 2024, gross profit is up +$32M year-on-year, but operating costs are up +$41M more than canceling out any benefit. Also, these quant scorings have very limited visibility into the hidden asset of the Melt pharma stake.

Covering Operational Costs (Triesence Launch)

Nonetheless, the top-line continues to accelerate with Iheezo and Vevye showing strong growth for Q3 2024 and significant potential for Triesence (appears to have launched as of Q4), which is not separately disclosed as it had not launched as of Q3. It's likely the marginal contribution for these products is at least 80%. The incremental revenue from these products in 2025 may more than justify HROW's current valuation and help justify the business model.

Product Estimated Incremental Revenue (2025) Contribution
Triesence +$100M +80M
Iheezo +$40M +$32M
Vevye +$20M +$16M
Total +$160M +$126M

The bulk of this comes from Triesence, as the company stated in their Q3 2024 10-Q: "In October 2024, we announced the re-launch of TRIESENCE following the successful manufacturing of three process performance qualification batches of the product". Triesence costs around $900. Then the CEO had this to say in a recent shareholder letter

The new Harrow corporate presentation attempts to describe why we remain bullish on TRIESENCE, the cornerstone product in the 2023 transaction with Novartis. Here’s why: TRIESENCE has a unique label and a product-specific reimbursement code (J-3300). TRIESENCE was always a high-utility product for U.S. ophthalmologists when it was available. Alternatives to TRIESENCE involve a potentially dangerous manipulation of preserved Kenalog, and we believe that U.S. ophthalmologists overwhelmingly prefer an on-label and reimbursed TRIESENCE over any other choice. Although about 86,000 TRIESENCE units were sold in 2018, the most recent annual period for which TRIESENCE was intermittently in stock, we estimate the current U.S. total addressable market exceeds 500,000 units annually.

Scenarios Manufacturing Failure 2018 sales level 25% market share 50% market share
Volume 0 86,000 125,000 250,000
Achieved price $800 $800 $800 $800
Contribution 0 $69M $100M $200M

Valuation

Element Free cashflow/Value Notes
Current run-rate of core business -$10M Average of Q1-Q3 2024 ex working capital (this is a bit of a disservice to a number of moving parts in the core business, which should improve in 2025, but they are less material)
Incremental contribution from growth of Triesence, Iheezo and Vevye +95M $126M above adjusted for tax
Resulting free cashflow +$85M Sum of above
Multiple 20x
Operating value $1.7B
Milestone payment for Triesence launch -$37M payment to Novartis
Legal settlement $35M legal case settled in HROW's favor - Nov '24
Possible value of HROW's 46% Melt stake $500M See analysis above
Net debt (Sept '24) -$114M
Stake in Surface Ophthalmics 0 May be upside here, but no clear commercial plans yet
Net Asset Position $384M
Total Value $2.085B
Shares out 38M Sept '24 is 35.6M but share-based compensation is dilutive
Value per share $54.85
Sensitivity: failure of Melt $41.71
Sensitivity: failure of Triesence $23.28

The above implies that HROW is likely reasonable value at current levels, assuming the Triesence launch goes as planned maintaining stated pricing and achieving 25% market share or a +46% uplift to the last known 2018 volume levels. The key being that Triesence provides sufficient gross margin to overcome the burden of operating costs, which appears likely given the now higher price point.

A successful Melt NDA filing is likely supportive of the valuation too, and perhaps worth ~$10/share to HROW. Of course, trends for Iheezo, Vevye etc. matter too but are less pressing for the valuation.

Risks

  • Clearly, HROW is not a widows and orphans bet. To the extent the stock succeeds in 2025 it will depend mainly on Triesence and Melt, the success of neither is guaranteed. Nonetheless, both may be considered more likely than not to succeed at this point.
  • The main risk is Triesence manufacturing fails since it is hard to manufacture, but so far, production appears to be progressing well as of Q3 disclosures.
  • If Melt were unable to file an NDA for their MELT-300 product, that would be a negative as it would if the application were rejected.
  • More broadly, operational costs at HROW must be contained, if operating costs continue to rise in lockstep with gross margin, then this business is going nowhere.
  • Further acquisitions in 2025 would almost certainly be a negative. Management has their hands full executing on prior acquisitions and further clouding the picture would be unwelcome.
  • Stock-based compensation is high and relatively dilutive, at current valuations this does not prevent equity upside, but it should be monitored as the company becomes more mature and larger in size.

Catalysts

  • Further evidence that Triesence can achieve 125,000+ units in 2025
  • Lack of surprises from other areas of the business with Iheezo and Vevye demonstrating continuing momentum
  • Evidence of operational leverage - gross margin clearly exceeding operating costs as 2025 progresses (likely related to the above factors)
  • Melt-300 progressing towards NDA filing in late 2025 and subsequent commercial launch in 2026

Conclusion

HROW currently trades at around $27/share it's not unrealistic to see the share price approximately doubling in 2025 if, primarily, the Triesence launch trends well and, secondarily, Melt tracks towards an NDA filing.

Other products in the company's portfolio are performing relatively well and strong execution on costs could yield further upside. In addition, the platform strategy of the business appears robust and if 2025 shows strong execution on past acquisitions, then continued execution of a similar strategy in 2026+ could cause the shares to compound further.
