data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Ardelyx, Aviva, Nutrien, Heartland Express +
data.description:
Undercovered Dozen Introduction

The 'Undercovered' Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this series provides ideas and inspires discussion.

Today, we're looking at articles published between February 21st - 27th.

Take a look at what these less-covered stock ideas might hold for you. And please join the conversation below: are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Rithm Capital Corp. Class A: When Preferred Shares Actually Get Called

Binary Tree Analytics | Buy

Rithm Capital Corp. (RITM) is a REIT which focuses on asset management and mortgages. The company has four series of preferred shares outstanding (Series A, Series B, Series C and Series D) most of which are fixed to floating re-setting.

The company just announced it is partially redeeming its Series A, Rithm Capital Corp. 7.50% PFD SER A (RITM.PR.A) of cumulative perpetual preferred shares, and we will focus on the implications from a duration and risk perspective. We believe that the current corporate action provides for a defined maturity profile for Series A, thus making them now attractive as a short-term investment instrument.

The redemption will occur on a non-standard payment date for the shares, which pay in February, May, August, and November. The March 2025 payment will be full principal and accrued interest to the call date. Kindly note that only 32% of the outstanding Series A will be called in March 2025. This translates into two-thirds of the preferred shares remaining outstanding.

When issued, they were perpetual, but the company has basically told investors that this is an expensive form of capital, and thus will be reduced going forward. This translates into a defined, reduced duration for the shares going forward. While nobody can ascertain for sure the exact remaining weighted average life here, we can safely say that Rithm will allocate capital to continue to pay down shares when it becomes available and the deployable projects do not meet an internal rate of return superior to the cost of funds for Series A.

Jumia's Business Prospects: Personal Reflections And Candid Conversation With CEO

Ahan Analytics | Strong Buy

There is perhaps nothing harder in investing than believing in a business and economic story that the market does not or cannot appreciate. I find myself at such a juncture with Jumia Technologies (JMIA). For two years, the company has worked diligently to restructure itself for efficiency, value, and to position for expansion into the future opportunity of e-commerce in Africa. With this work now bearing the fruit of definitive year-long guidance and the outlines of a path to profitability, the stock has seen fit to price in failure. Where the market sees struggle, I see the profile of a company made resilient and robust for the rigors of African marketplaces. While the stock market stews over its angst on JMIA, I continue to tell the story in the expectation of better performance in the future.

This latest piece on JMIA covers four components: insights from my latest interview with CEO Francis Dufay including results from earnings and the accompanying conference call, my observations from visiting Jumia's warehouse in Lagos, a "man on the street" discussion about the benefits of shopping with Jumia in Lagos, Nigeria, and a qualitative assessment of the macroeconomic environment in Nigeria.

During the interview, I asked Dufay the question that should be top of mind for any long-term investor absorbing the current losses: the stock price is incredibly low given the long-term potential for Jumia, how do you describe what the market is missing?

Dufay started his answer by acknowledging that "the market is always right." However, he reiterated "the big news" - Jumia is back to growth by benefiting from a clear strategy focused on up-country, value formation, better customer experience, and market efficiency. He underlined the effort involved by explaining this is "healthy stuff and hard work." For the first time in a long time with very low marketing cost, Jumia is expanding the customer base by 8% and growing physical orders by 18% (year-over-year for Q4). In other words, the "health KPIs are turning green, really green."

WELL Health: Spin-Offs & Strategic Clarity Make This A Buy Ahead Of Earnings

Nikola Lapenna | Buy

WELL Health Technologies Corp. (WELL:CA)(OTC:WHTCF), headquartered in Vancouver, is a leading Canadian digital health company that is focused on modernizing and improving healthcare through various technology means. WELL operates a portfolio of primary care clinics, telehealth platforms, electronic medical record solutions, and cybersecurity tools for the healthcare sector. The company has been extremely active in relation to mergers & acquisitions, closing dozens of deals in 2024, and has several LOIs in the pipeline, along with spin-off plans.

WELL recently had a run-up after strong Q3 earnings and provided more clarity on its spinoff plans for WELLSTAR. That subsidiary will be spun-off independently, and they raised $50MM from institutional investors to scale. Ultimately, I think that this stock has been stuck in the mud for over a year due to a lack of understanding from investors. While these moves have been a long time coming, the company is set up for a strong 2025 as their revenues and earnings will continue to soar and clarity on the company's strategy will come into focus. Without any pure play peers, I rate the stock as a Buy with a price target of $10 using both a DCF valuation and a sum of the parts multiple.

WELL will report Q4 results in late March, and all eyes will be on the company's plan to reshape its operations. I recommend investors look for a couple key things in the upcoming earnings. The first is its progress on its spin-off strategy. While WELLSTAR was announced as being spun-off in December, WELL will still have a significant stake in the company. I'm curious to hear how management expects these moves to create value and how WELL will continue to exert influence over the independent company as the majority owning shareholder. Additionally, management has been soured on its stock price and believes that additional value can be created through spin-offs of CRH Medical and better integration of Healwell AI (AIDX:CA), which they expect to buy out fully in 2025.

Aviva: Direct Line Expected Acquisition Doesn't Change Its Income Appeal

Labutes IR | Hold

As I’ve covered in previous articles, Aviva (OTCPK:AIVAF) is an interesting income pick in the European insurance sector, due to its high-dividend yield that seems to be sustainable over the long term. Since my previous analysis on the company, Aviva has decided to make an offer to buy its competitor Direct Line (OTCPK:DIISF), a competitor that I have also covered some months ago.

This offer was successful, and Aviva is expected to complete this acquisition over the coming months, in a deal valued at about $4.6 billion. Given that Aviva’s current market value is around $16 billion, this deal is quite significant and is expected to have a significant impact on the company’s profile following the integration of Direct Line.

Aviva has been on a transformation phase over the past few years, as the company refocused its operations in three core markets, namely the U.K., Canada, and Ireland. Other geographies were sold, as Aviva was not able to reach the desired size or have a leadership position, preferring to streamline its operations in markets where it has some sort of competitive advantage.

Moreover, its strategy is to shift the business toward capital-light activities across insurance, wealth and retirement segments, shifting its business away from life insurance toward other areas of the industry. In recent years, Aviva has performed several small bolt-on acquisitions in wealth or property & casualty (P&C) insurance, but the acquisition of Direct Line will have a much greater impact on its operations.

Ardelyx: Limited Pipeline, Rising Costs, And Uncertain Growth Ahead (Downgrade)

Stephen Ayers | Sell

Ardelyx, Inc.’s (ARDX) stock is up a modest 4% since my last evaluation in November. I recommended “hold,” but expressed caution due to “regulatory headwinds, competitive pressures, and uncertain long-term growth beyond tenapanor.” Back then, the focus was on its hyperphosphatemia drug, Xphozah (tenapanor). As of January 1st, Xphozah is no longer reimbursed by Medicare Part D.

Ardelyx also chose not to apply for a Transitional Drug Add-on Payment Adjustment, or TDAPA, effectively forgoing Medicare Part D reimbursement. As of March 2023, over 51 million Americans were enrolled in Medicare Part D. Roughly 80% of dialysis patients, Xphozah’s target market, are enrolled in Medicare Part D.

Ardelyx believes Xphozah can achieve $750 million in annual U.S. net product sales before patent expiration. However, the bundling means that dialysis centers will receive a fixed payment for all services and medications. This may limit their willingness to adopt newer and more expensive therapies like Xphozah. The drug, launched in Q4 2023, delivered $160.9 million in sales in the full year 2024 and $57.2 million in Q4.

Recall that Ardelyx also markets tenapanor as Ibsrela for IBS-C. Ibsrela is one of many drugs utilized for constipation, so it faces a lot of competition. Ardelyx believes that Ibsrela can deliver $1 billion in annual sales at its peak based on the assumption that Ibsrela can capture 10% market share. The drug brought in $158.3 million in full year 2024 and $53.8 million in Q4 (up 32% Q/Q).

In the days following Ardelyx’s Q4 earnings report on February 20, its stock fell considerably.

Udemy On The Rise: Inside Its Game-Changing Strategy To Dominate EdTech

Weebler Finance | Strong Buy

Udemy, Inc. (UDMY) is a $1.5 billion edtech company that operates an online marketplace platform connecting consumers with tens of thousands of experts in different subject matters for the purposes of skill acquisition, development, as well as validation.

On Friday last week, Udemy released its Q4 and full 2024 results, and the market was clearly impressed, considering that the stock underwent a single-day jump of over 28%. While revenue in 2024 jumped by about 8%, the enterprise side of the business actually climbed by an impressive 18%. Adjusted EBITDA jumped from $7.8 million in 2023 to a staggering $43 million in 2024. The company further expects this figure to jump to as high as $85 million by the end of 2025. For context, as recently as in 2022, Udemy reported an adjusted EBITDA loss of almost $50 million. This achievement really is significant, and demonstrates excellent execution on the part of the company's management (which owns over 47% of the company!)

In the wake of the AI proliferation boom, and the dynamic shifts impacting conventional educational landscapes, the entire space Udemy functions within is being drastically redefined. As a result, Udemy is shifting gears, and changing its strategy to increase its focus on the enterprise side of its business, whilst simultaneously refining its products across the platform through AI-powered tools.

With massive growth potential, and strong fundamentals, I believe Udemy is a steal at its current price.

Chartwell Retirement Residences: Leading The Sector Rebound

The Affluent Tortoise | Buy

After a sharp decline during the COVID-19 pandemic, retirement REITs have largely recovered. Since I last recommended Chartwell Retirement Residences (CSH.UN:CA), (OTC:CWSRF) in May 2023, units are up 80%, well ahead of the S&P 500’s return of 47% over the same period. Despite the impressive rebound, there are several catalysts to support additional upside for this REIT.

Chartwell is a pure play private retirement operator. The company’s portfolio of 27,000 mid-market and upscale retirement suites are located in urban and suburban sites across Alberta, British Columbia, Ontario and Quebec.

As Canada’s largest provider of seniors housing, this REIT is well positioned to capitalize on prevailing demographic trends in Canada. Over the next 20 years, the number of 80+ year-olds in Canada is forecast to grow at a compound annual rate of 4.3%. Another tailwind that should continue to drive a recovery in occupancy levels is a favourable demand-supply paradigm resulting from a highly constrained supply of new senior housing units being developed.

As a market leader with high-quality assets, an attractive market segment and a well diversified geographic footprint, Chartwell is well positioned to capitalize on the demographic opportunity in seniors housing. Over the last 24 months, occupancy has improved by nearly 10% as Chartwell has optimized its portfolio, acquiring premium properties and divesting its aging inventory. With its above-average leverage, Chartwell will benefit from falling interest rates.

The Other Five Fit For Mention

Heartland Express Could Double In The Next Cycle

Deep Value Searcher | Buy

Heartland Express, Inc. (HTLD) could double in 2-3 years as freight rates stabilize and the company continues deleveraging and integrating recent acquisitions. Despite a 47.42% decline in share price, Heartland Express is poised for a turnaround due to effective management and a cyclical upswing in the trucking industry. The company has reduced over $300 million in debt since 2022, aiming for a debt-free balance sheet, which should significantly de-risk the business. With a historically modest 15x FCF or PE multiple, Heartland Express could see an 81% upside, translating to an annualized return of over 22%.

Nutrien And U.S. Tariffs: No Overreaction, No Buying Opportunity

Hong Chew Eu | Hold

Nutrien Ltd. (NTR)(NTR:CA) faces potential 25% US tariffs on Canadian imports, increasing costs by 3% and reducing intrinsic value by one-third, making it unattractive at current prices. The valuation shows a substantial reduction in intrinsic value due to tariffs, with a base case value of $92 per share dropping to $62 per share. Nutrien was formed in 2018 by merging two major Canadian agricultural companies, creating one of the world's largest fertilizer producers. According to the company, it now operates the largest global network of fertilizer production and distribution assets. Given the market has not yet factored in the tariff impact, I do not recommend buying Nutrien stock at this time.

Oatly’s Oatmilk Bubble: Why The Once-Hot Brand May Be Fizzling Out

Muslim Farooque | Sell

Oatly Group AB (OTLY) was an early mover in the plant-based dairy space, specializing in oat-based products. From its humble beginnings in Sweden back in 1994, Oatly has become one of the global leaders in oat-based products including oat milk, yogurts (“oatgurts”), ice creams, spreads, and much more. Their stock has plummeted nearly 65% in the past year, facing secular decline and weak demand, making it unsuitable for long-term investors. Despite some financial improvements, Oatly's liquidity remains poor, with a cash-to-debt ratio of 0.2 and a debt-to-equity ratio of 4.69. Oatly's valuation appears expensive, trading at six times 2025 sales estimates, with modest growth projections and a slight turn to positive EBITDA.

Allogene Therapeutics: Disrupting CAR-T With A Scalable Model

Equity Eagle | Buy

Allogene Therapeutics, Inc. (ALLO) appears undervalued despite market skepticism, with a robust cash runway and potential breakthroughs in allogeneic CAR-T therapies. The company's lead asset, cema-cel, aims to shift CAR-T therapy to early intervention, potentially expanding its market and redefining treatment standards. ALLO-329 targets autoimmune diseases, positioning Allogene in a new market with significant unmet needs and less competition compared to oncology. Key risks include the efficacy and durability of allogeneic CAR-T therapies and manufacturing consistency, but early data and strategic positioning offer compelling upside potential.

DXC Technology: IT Is Withering Away

Joseph Parrish | Hold

DXC Technology Company's stock (DXC) has seen a negative 40% return over the past decade, despite the generative AI boom. The company's shrinking fundamentals, driven by market pressures and competitive IT space, show ongoing revenue decline and a lack of AI capitalization. Capital allocation issues, including high buybacks during peak stock prices and significant debt, further complicate DXC's financial stability. While a low free cash flow multiple is tempting, the risk of bankruptcy due to debt repayment challenges makes the stock unattractive without a significant price drop.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: How I Would Retire On A $1 Million REIT Portfolio
data.description:
Co-produced by Austin Rogers

So you want to retire early?

While it's totally understandable to yearn for freedom from the day-to-day grind, grueling commutes, annoying bosses or coworkers, and lack of leisure time, there are lots and lots of things to consider before actually pulling the trigger to retire early.

Chief among those questions to ask is, "Can I afford it?"

In 2023, Bureau of Labor Statistics data showed that retiree households (over age 65) spent an average of $60,087, while the average spending across all households was $77,280. But much of the average retiree household's spending is on healthcare. Presumably, the average early retiree will not immediately need to spend as much as older households on medical costs, so income needs should be lower.

Plus, by the time the typical retiree begins to need more healthcare services, they will usually have begun collecting Social Security benefits, which adds about $22,000 on average to annual income.

Let's say you have a nest egg of $1 million. Could you comfortably retire early on that amount by living solely off the income while not touching the principal?

Yes, we believe you could.

One way to do this would be to concentrate one's investment in real estate investment trusts ("REITs"), which are designed to be income-generating investment vehicles.

Let's explore.

Why REITs? Why Today?

To be clear, we do not recommend retiring 100% on REITs. As Warren Buffett once said, diversification is the only free lunch in investing, and no sector of the market is attractive enough to deserve 100% of one's investable cash.

We discuss the idea of investing $1 million into REITs as either a hypothetical or as a possibility for someone with well over $1 million in total investable assets.

Why look at REITs for retirement? The reason is obvious. REITs are designed to provide high income by requiring companies to pay out at least 90% of taxable income in exchange for a full pass-through of taxation to shareholders (no corporate tax).

REITs offer yields typically ranging from 3-6%, compared to the S&P 500's (SPY) dividend yield of about 1.2%.

Moreover, due to the post-COVID inflationary surge and subsequent rapid rise in interest rates, REITs have become around the most discounted relative to the S&P 500 that they've ever been in the last 20 years.

While this chart ends at the end of 2024, the huge discount likely has not changed much year-to-date, as the S&P 500 has performed about as well as REITs so far this year.

This huge valuation discount should provide some comfort to investors, both because it has resulted in higher dividend yields and because it creates far less risk of multiple contraction for REITs than for the broader stock market.

Why Not Buy A REIT ETF?

One idea to invest in REITs is through a passive, low-cost ETF like the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ), which charges an expense ratio of only 0.13%, or the SPDR Real Estate ETF (XLRE), which charges only 0.09%.

While these are low fees, there are many good reasons not to invest in a passive REIT ETF.

First, dividend yields are relatively low. For example, VNQ yields 3.8%, while XLRE yields 3.4%. We believe that investors who do their research and buy on dips can achieve a meaningfully higher portfolio-wide yield than this.

Second, real estate is the most passively owned sector of the stock market.

Why is this?

Most stock investors are generalists who understand the average business model of selling goods or services but do not have expertise in the particular business model of commercial real estate.

CRE experts mostly work in the CRE industry, leaving the stock market disproportionately populated by non-CRE-experts. These folks generally recognize their lack of expertise in CRE and opt for passive REIT exposure via ETFs.

Since passive ETFs are market cap-weighted, high passive ownership has the effect of channeling a huge amount of capital into the biggest REITs. But the biggest REITs are simply those with the largest real estate portfolios, not necessarily the most earnings or fastest earnings growth - and definitely not the highest dividend yields.

To some degree, we think this creates some misallocation of capital in the REIT space, overly rewarding big REITs and overly punishing smaller REITs.

On the other hand, this does result in some great buying opportunities among small- and mid-cap REITs.

More so than any other sector of the stock market, active managers in the real estate space mostly outperform their passive index benchmarks.

For example, the ALPS Active REIT ETF (REIT), Janus Henderson US Real Estate ETF (JRE) and JPMorgan Realty Income ETF (JPRE) are all active REIT ETFs that have outperformed the VNQ since their inception several years ago:

The bad news is that investors have to pay up for this outperformance.

REIT's expense ratio is 0.68%, while JRE's sits at 0.65% and JPRE's at 0.5%. For a $1 million portfolio, that represents annual fees of between $5,000 and $6,800.

How To Build A $1 Million Early Retirement Portfolio

If we wanted to retire early on a $1 million REIT portfolio, here's how we would do so.

First off, we would include REIT preferred stocks, which are basically a hybrid between equity and debt and offer high but fixed dividend income.

Here's how we would generally arrange the portfolio:

REIT Common Stocks 75% Weighting 5% Average Yield
REIT Preferred Stocks 25% Weighting 7.5% Average Yield

This hypothetical portfolio would yield about 5.6%, which means that $1 million invested in it would generate a little over $56,000 in annual dividend income.

Obviously, one could tweak the weightings to adjust the level of income or growth potential.

While REIT common stocks offer the potential of income growth, preferred stocks' primary benefit is their high current income.

Note that we're assuming an average dividend yield of 5% for REIT common stocks. This is above average but does not imply that one will have to invest in a bunch of high-yielding REITs with dividends at high risk of being cut. There are plenty of solid, steadily growing REITs with yields of about 5%, plus or minus a percentage point.

Here are some examples:

REIT Yield Credit Rating
Agree Realty (ADC) 4.2% BBB+
Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) 5.5% BBB+
CareTrust REIT (CTRE) 4.3% BB+
Healthpeak Properties (DOC) 6.1% BBB+
Extra Space Storage (EXR) 4.2% BBB+
Federal Realty Trust (FRT) 4.0% BBB+
NNN REIT (NNN) 6.0% BBB+
Rexford Industrial (REXR) 4.1% BBB+
W.P. Carey (WPC) 6.3% BBB+
VICI Properties (VICI) 5.8% BBB-

Notice that while these REITs offer an average yield of about 5%, they almost all offer investment grade credit ratings, signifying the strength of their balance sheets. The only REIT above with a sub-IG rating (CareTrust REIT) currently has virtually zero debt after spending last year equitizing its balance sheet.

How about the preferred stock portion of the portfolio? How reasonable is it to assume an average yield of 7.5% for this piece?

We think it's relatively easy right now to assemble a 7.5%-yielding basket of REIT prefs. Here are some examples:

REIT Preferred Stock Dividend Yield
Arbor Realty Fixed-to-Floating Series F (ABR.PR.F) 7.0%
Gladstone Commercial Series G (GOODO) 7.2%
EPR Properties Series G (EPR.PR.G) 7.3%
Gladstone Land Series B (LANDO) 7.4%
Armada Hoffler Properties Series A (AHH.PR.A) 7.6%
KKR Real Estate Finance Series A (KREF.PR.A) 8.3%
Innovative Industrial Properties Series A (IIPR.PR.A) 8.7%

These preferred stocks are inherently safer and less volatile than the common stocks of their issuers. And all but IIPR.PR.A trade at discounts to their redemption value of $25 per share.

And importantly, each of these prefs maintained their dividends through the very difficult period of COVID-19, illustrating the stability of their dividend income streams.

Bottom Line

Retiring early on a $1 million REIT portfolio is entirely possible but, as with everything in investing, should be done with as much thought and planning as possible. And as with anything in life in which one is not personally an expert, it never hurts to consult experts.

At High Yield Landlord, we maintain a high-yielding, low turnover Retirement Portfolio designed to maximize safe income while secondarily providing meaningful upside to fair value.

data.title: Tucows' Liquidity Squeeze Made It Stop CAPEX, The Name Is Very Risky
data.description:
Tucows (TCX) reported 4Q24 results and held an 'earnings recording'.

The company's financials are highly challenged, with liquidity risk increasing this quarter as well. In 3Q24 the company announced that it would decrease the build-out of fiber to save on cash at the consolidated and off-balance sheet level. This indicates the degree of liquidity stress at the company.

Tucows guided for a year with about $56 million in adjusted EBITDA, not enough to cover interest after other expenses. It will be very hard for the company to maintain operations without new financing.

For that reason, I maintain my Hold on the name.

4Q24 results and situation

A curious 'call': Instead of holding an earnings call like most companies of its size do, Tucows releases a recorded reading without a live Q&A session. Analysts and shareholders send questions, which management filters and answers almost a month later. This is the only company I know of that has this type of earnings call.

Ting 3Q24 capital plan restructuring: In 3Q24 Tucows announced that it would stop building the fiber infrastructure of Ting, its fiber business, because of the capital pressure on the business (both at the consolidated and subsidiary level).

In 4Q24 the company reduced 'organic' CAPEX to $4.8 million (actual figure was ~$12 million but because of cash payments). The company says this is the new yearly figure, which is previously what one quarter of CAPEX looked like.

The new plan also implies that Ting will not add new serviceable addresses by itself (potentially it can via partners), and therefore it will have lower growth going forward.

The reduction in CAPEX also generated a reduction in OpEx, and Ting is expected to reach adjusted EBITDA breakeven in 2025, after moving from ~$10 million per quarter adjusted EBITDA loss in 1H24 to 'only' $1.5 million loss in 4Q24.

Liquidity situation is bad: At the consolidated level (4Q24 short form financial statements, 10-K has not been filed yet), Tucows has $57 million in cash, versus $200 million in current liabilities (customer deposits of $135 million being the largest account), and $600 million in debt and preferred instruments.

These figures include Ting's subsidiary, which is bankruptcy remote but consolidated. Without the 10-K it is difficult to understand Ting's financials but as of 3Q24 10-Q, Ting represented about $400 million in debt and preferreds, had $62 million in cash (total company cash in Q4 is lower) and had $40 million in interest payment commitments in the NTM from September 2024.

Even if the company manages to generate $56 million in adjusted EBITDA (double adjusted because without the second adjustment it would be $46 million, because of a payment to an MVNO), it would only barely cover interest of $56 million, without considering CAPEX of $20 million, plus additional expenses in cash not considered in adjusted EBITDA.

Too risky for investment

Without equity or debt financing, Tucows' liquidity is too fragile. The company recognized this because it had to reduce CAPEX to one quarter for Tucows.

In order to survive, Tucows needs to turn its whole operating break even in 2025. It is very unclear whether this can happen at all. Particularly, it is unclear how much can Ting grow without expanding its serviceable addresses.

A company that is losing so much money, that is at such a level of financial risk, that has shown terrible capital allocation mechanisms, that is not clear in its reporting practices, with insiders selling the up the same day of the earnings release, and with little insider ownership, cannot be considered an investment, not even in speculative terms.

Risks are too high, because unless Tucows reaches more than break even, then the equity can very possibly be worth $0.

For that reason, I maintain my Hold on the name.
